assumption_text: The episode theater is limited to Dhaka/Dacca urban core and immediate suburbs directly covered by Operation Searchlight tasking, not all of East Pakistan/Bangladesh.
rationale: Sources define this as a city-and-suburbs assault; a whole-country denominator would violate the project rule against whole-country exposure for a city episode.

assumption_text: Civilian exposure is reduced by immediate flight. I model average in-episode outflow at 120,000 (low denominator), 75,000 (best), and 30,000 (high denominator).
rationale: ICJ reports that tens of thousands had fled Dacca within days; no daily administrative count was found.

assumption_text: Total organized-armed personnel directly exposed in the Dhaka episode are modeled at 6,000 / 7,500 / 9,500 over the seven-day episode.
rationale: This range reflects four named West Pakistani infantry battalions plus support units, combined with Bengali organized-armed personnel in the city, while preserving the Farman-vs-Salik disagreement over EPR and police headcounts.

assumption_text: University student deaths are held in unknown status rather than silently folded into civilians or military.
rationale: The direct death range exists, but sources differ on whether some halls returned fire or possessed weapons; a conservative unknown bucket is preferable to false precision.

assumption_text: Seven calendar days of exposure are converted to person-months using a 7/30 factor.
rationale: The required metric is person-month exposure, and the episode is intentionally short and bounded.

assumption_text: Residual civilian deaths are computed from an all-status direct-death envelope after subtracting military and unknown-status deaths, not from mixed mortality or indirect mortality claims.
rationale: The best aggregate Dhaka total is direct violent death from military action; using a residual inside that direct-death universe is permissible and more transparent than importing whole-war mixed mortality numbers.
